food_name: Bean Sprouts, Blanched Mung Bean (Vigna radiata)

local_names.romanized_local: Taugeh rebus (Malay); Dou ya chao shui (Chinese romanized)

region_of_origin: South Asia (Vigna radiata native to India); widely cultivated across Southeast Asia and East Asia
food_subgroup: Blanched mung bean sprouts (Vigna radiata); very low calorie, very low potassium, CKD-safe vegetable

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a mung bean sprout entry; closest match used for blanched/cooked form as no exact blanched-specific term exists.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mung bean sprouts (Vigna radiata), cooked, blanched
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00BS used as closest parent for blanched mung bean sprouts; exact blanched sub-facet not available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for blanched mung bean sprouts as a food item.

serving.description: 1 generous hawker garnish (~80g) or 100g reference portion
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard 100g reference portion of blanched mung bean sprouts, equivalent to approximately one generous hawker-stall garnish of about 80g as served alongside laksa, Hokkien mee, or char kway teow.

nutrient_source.title: Bean Sprouts Mung Cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #11043
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, SR Legacy Foods Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy #11043 reports mung bean sprouts, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt — directly applicable to blanched, drained preparation used in Singapore hawker context.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mung Bean Sprouts — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for mung beans and sprouted legumes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / peer-reviewed literature on sprouted legume glycaemic response
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~25 estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked mung beans (GI ~31) and sprouted/germinated legumes, which consistently show lower GI than whole cooked legumes due to partial hydrolysis of starch during germination and high fibre content. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (25 × 4.9 × 1.0) / 100 ≈ 1.2 per 100g reference; GL per 80g hawker serving ≈ 0.7. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (6 mg/100g); modest potassium (149 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: With only 6 mg sodium per 100g, blanched mung bean sprouts are an excellent low-sodium vegetable for hypertensive patients and can be consumed freely as a garnish or side without contributing to sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~25), negligible glycemic load, high fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 25 and a glycemic load of under 1 per serving, blanched mung bean sprouts produce minimal postprandial glucose excursion and are suitable for unrestricted inclusion in a Type 2 Diabetes meal plan; folate (63 mcg DFE/100g) also addresses the elevated folate requirements common in T2DM patients.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0 g), negligible cholesterol (0 mg), low calorie density (26 kcal/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Blanched mung bean sprouts contain no saturated fat and no cholesterol, making them a heart-healthy vegetable that supports weight management and cardiovascular risk reduction when used as a low-calorie meal extender.

chronic_kidney_disease.key_factor: Very low potassium (149 mg/100g); low phosphorus (55 mg/100g)
chronic_kidney_disease.guidance: Blanched mung bean sprouts are CKD-safe with only 149 mg potassium and 55 mg phosphorus per 100g; no potassium restriction is required and they can be freely used as a garnish or vegetable component in CKD meal plans.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Folate (63 mcg DFE/100g) supports methotrexate-treated patients; low oxalate
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Folate content (63 mcg DFE/100g) is clinically relevant for RA patients on methotrexate therapy; vitamin C (8 mg/100g) supports collagen synthesis and joint tissue antioxidant defence, and low oxalate content makes bean sprouts suitable for RA patients also at risk for kidney stones.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Blanched mung bean sprouts (Vigna radiata) are the germinated shoots of the mung bean, briefly immersed in boiling water and drained, yielding a tender-crisp white shoot with a pale yellow tip and a mild, fresh, slightly grassy flavour. Per 100g blanched and drained: approximately 26 kcal (very low calorie density), 2.5g protein, 0.1g fat, 4.9g carbohydrate, 1.9g dietary fibre, and only 6mg sodium. Potassium is very low at 149mg/100g, making this one of the few vegetables that is unambiguously CKD-safe without portion restriction. Glycemic index is approximately 25 (low), with a negligible glycemic load of under 1 per serving, making it ideal for Type 2 Diabetes meal plans. Germination reduces phytate content by 50–75% relative to dry mung beans and increases vitamin C approximately 8-fold. Folate content (63 mcg DFE/100g) is clinically relevant for patients on methotrexate (RA) and for T2DM patients with elevated folate requirements. In Singapore hawker culture, taugeh (bean sprouts) is an ubiquitous garnish and ingredient in laksa, Hokkien mee, char kway teow, and mee rebus. Food safety note: blanching rather than serving raw is recommended for immunocompromised patients. Source: USDA SR Legacy #11043 (mung bean sprouts, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt). Confidence: HIGH.
common_user_queries: 豆芽 kidney disease CKD potassium
mung bean sprouts vs soy sprouts

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy #11043 (mung bean sprouts, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt), which is an exact match for the blanched, drained preparation. All core macros and secondary nutrients are fully populated from this approved primary source. GI estimated at 0.9 confidence from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for mung beans and sprouted legumes.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for cooked mung bean sprouts is not separately reported in USDA SR Legacy #11043 (total fat is 0.1g, making fatty acid sub-fractions analytically negligible). water_g set to -1: moisture data not extracted from USDA SR Legacy #11043 for this record. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy #11043. cholesterol_mg is confirmed 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived food product. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived food product. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived food product with no UV exposure during germination. saturated_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy #11043 (total fat 0.1g, saturated fat below detection threshold). GI value of ~25 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked mung beans and sprouted legumes; no direct GI measurement for blanched mung bean sprouts was located in the University of Sydney database as of April 2026. Glycemic load per 100g reference serving = (25 × 4.9) / 100 = 1.2; GL per 80g hawker garnish serving ≈ 0.7 as reported. FoodEx2 code A00BS used as closest parent; exact blanched sub-facet not available in FoodEx2 hierarchy. Record type: Generic. Data: per 100g blanched, drained, without salt.
